category_summary:
The AI community was captivated by two major storylines: **GPT-5.4 Pro**'s claimed breakthroughs in mathematics and **Anthropic**'s alarming safety research on subliminal learning in LLMs.

- **Greg Brockman** [shared details on](/?date=2026-04-16&category=social#item-401bcf4dc61f) **GPT-5.4 Pro** making 'beautiful contributions to mathematics,' comparing them to discovering new chess opening lines overlooked by humans. **Tunguz** and others [validated the claims](/?date=2026-04-16&category=social#item-6723719ab550) as genuinely novel, not mere pattern matching.
- **Anthropic** [published research in **Nature**](/?date=2026-04-16&category=social#item-b5f66c2f24e9) showing LLMs can pass on traits like misalignment through hidden signals — a landmark AI safety finding generating massive engagement (1764 likes, 212K views).
- **Google** [launched **Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS**](/?date=2026-04-16&category=social#item-e96cb3113d01) with novel Audio Tags for natural-language voice direction, supporting 70+ languages with **SynthID** watermarking.
- **Ethan Mollick** [described a recurring pattern](/?date=2026-04-16&category=social#item-f414221896ac) where AI breakthroughs get lost between overstated claims and minor wins. **John Carmack** offered [a unique lens on LLM training](/?date=2026-04-16&category=social#item-abfb7200ae26) as near-lossless compression of massive corpora.
- **natolambert** shared [a comprehensive framework](/?date=2026-04-16&category=social#item-ba28664b5101) on open vs. closed models, noting closed models surprisingly haven't pulled ahead despite compute advantages. **Andrew Ng** [launched a new course](/?date=2026-04-16&category=social#item-1e36f8f0b68b) on spec-driven development with coding agents via **JetBrains**.

1. It’s surprising that the top closed models did not show a growing capability margin over open models, based on compute differences for training and research, especially in the second half of 2025 and through today.

items.title: OK, this is super important and super interesting. This validation from someone who’s highly experie...
items.content: OK, this is super important and super interesting. This validation from someone who’s highly experienced and skilled in advanced mathematics is extremely convincing to me. It seems that these AI tools are *really* coming up with new and useful mathematics. It’s not just fortuitous pattern matching for low hanging fruits that were possible to do for professional mathematicians but were otherwise overlooked. The fact that they are actually doing all of this with a model that’s widely available - ChatGPT 5.4 - is quite remarkable in its own right. The rest of this year is going to be wild.

items.title:
Document OCR benchmarks are still an open problem

Existing document OCR benchmarks are either too n...
items.content:
Document OCR benchmarks are still an open problem

Existing document OCR benchmarks are either too narrowly focused on a specific type (e.g. FinTabNet, ChartQA), or on documents that aren’t reflective of real-world tasks (e.g. OmniDocBench, OlmOCR-bench on over academic papers)

ParseBench is a step towards solving this problem. 
* It tries to comprehensively cover real-world document distributions within the enterprise.
* It contains comprehensive evaluations across 5 different dimensions (tables, charts, content faithfulness, formatting, grounding). 
* It tries to use metrics that optimize for agent semantic understanding rather than structural similarity.

We released this yesterday, and there’s a TON of content:
1. Whitepaper
2. HF dataset
3. Github repo
4. Blog
5. Video

And today, we’re excited to feature https://t.co/FYbk3s6M2w, our home page website for ParseBench 💫 come check it out!

Take a look at some of our other materials if you’re interested:
Blog: https://t.co/57OHkx0pQW
Paper: https://t.co/Ho2oH2xEAM

items.title: It is generally frowned upon to have LLMs precisely regurgitate part of their training set, but it i...
items.content:
It is generally frowned upon to have LLMs precisely regurgitate part of their training set, but it is an interesting question how you could use LLM training to nearly losslesly compress a huge corpus like the entirety of the Internet Archive.

The Hutter Prize is for perfect compression, but only one GB. There would be different trades at the PB level, and it gets much more interesting when it doesn’t have to be bit-accurate.
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack discusses using LLM training as near-lossless compression for massive corpora like the Internet Archive. References the Hutter Prize (1GB perfect compression) and speculates about PB-scale trades where bit-accuracy isn't required.
items.reasoning: Highly original technical insight from John Carmack (legendary programmer). 969 likes, 71K views. Explores novel intersection of LLMs and compression theory at scale. Thought-provoking theoretical question with practical implications.
items.summary_html: John Carmack discusses using LLM training as near-lossless compression for massive corpora like the Internet Archive. References the Hutter Prize (1GB perfect compression) and speculates about PB-scale trades where bit-accuracy isn't required.
items.content_html:
It is generally frowned upon to have LLMs precisely regurgitate part of their training set, but it is an interesting question how you could use LLM training to nearly losslesly compress a huge corpus like the entirety of the Internet Archive.

The Hutter Prize is for perfect compression, but only one GB. There would be different trades at the PB level, and it gets much more interesting when it doesn’t have to be bit-accurate.

items.title: New course: Spec-Driven Development with Coding Agents, built in partnership with @jetbrains, and ta...
items.content:
New course: Spec-Driven Development with Coding Agents, built in partnership with @jetbrains, and taught by @paulweveritt.

Vibe coding is fast, but often produces code that doesn't match what you asked for. This short course teaches you spec-driven development: write a detailed spec defining what to build, and work with your coding agent to implement it. Many of the best developers already build this way.

A spec lets you control large code changes with a few words, preserve context across agent sessions, and stay in control as your project grows in complexity.

Skills you'll gain:
- Write a detailed specification to define your mission, tech stack, and roadmap, giving your agent the context it needs from the start
- Plan, implement, and validate features in iterative loops using a spec as your agent's guide
- Apply the same repeatable workflow to both new and legacy codebases
- Package your workflow into a portable agent skill that works across agents and IDEs

Join and write specs that keep your coding agent on track!
https://t.co/hI4GwuvhtN

items.title:
At vLLM Korea Meetup 2026 (Seoul, April 2), engineers shared how they run vLLM in production:

- Sam...
items.content:
At vLLM Korea Meetup 2026 (Seoul, April 2), engineers shared how they run vLLM in production:

- Samsung: air-gapped private LLM API on internal GPUs, serving 4,000+ employees
- NAVER Cloud: disaggregated serving for HyperCLOVA Omni, 3x latency reduction
- Upstage: taking Solar LLM from open-source weights to production service on vLLM, with token-level generation control

Thanks to Rebellionsai, SqueezeBitsAI, @RedHatAPAC, and @PyTorch Korea for co-hosting.

Full writeup 🔗 https://t.co/xKMs8SY7In

items.summary: vLLM project shares highlights from their Korea Meetup: Samsung running air-gapped LLM for 4000+ employees, NAVER Cloud achieving 3x latency reduction with disaggregated serving for HyperCLOVA, and Upstage's production Solar LLM deployment.
items.reasoning: Excellent production deployment insights from major Korean companies. Samsung's enterprise LLM deployment, NAVER's disaggregated serving architecture, and practical vLLM usage patterns are all highly relevant. Strong engagement (97 likes, 13K views).
items.summary_html: vLLM project shares highlights from their Korea Meetup: Samsung running air-gapped LLM for 4000+ employees, NAVER Cloud achieving 3x latency reduction with disaggregated serving for HyperCLOVA, and Upstage's production Solar LLM deployment.
items.content_html:
At vLLM Korea Meetup 2026 (Seoul, April 2), engineers shared how they run vLLM in production:

  • Samsung: air-gapped private LLM API on internal GPUs, serving 4,000+ employees
  • NAVER Cloud: disaggregated serving for HyperCLOVA Omni, 3x latency reduction
  • Upstage: taking Solar LLM from open-source weights to production service on vLLM, with token-level generation control

Thanks to Rebellionsai, SqueezeBitsAI, @RedHatAPAC, and @PyTorch Korea for co-hosting.

Full writeup 🔗 https://t.co/xKMs8SY7In

items.title: Obviously, models are a big deal, but coding harnesses play a huge role in making these models look ...
items.content:
Obviously, models are a big deal, but coding harnesses play a huge role in making these models look good.

I suspect that you can get the best frontier model out there, put it in a shitty harness, and the experience will be very disappointing.

The reverse is also true: put a mediocre model in a strong harness, and it might match the experience that you get from the best agentic coding tools out there.

So, yes, Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.3-Codex are amazing models, but the Claude Code and Codex harnesses do a lot of the lifting to make them work the way they are.

Of course, these models might also have some specific training on their harnesses. This is also an advantage.

items.reasoning: Insightful analysis of harness vs model contribution to coding tool quality, names specific frontier models, good engagement (10K views), relevant to ongoing debate about what drives coding AI quality

items.title:
proud to see @excalidraw evangelism catching on at cog

the insight here is more general than bugche...
items.content:
proud to see @excalidraw evangelism catching on at cog

the insight here is more general than bugchecking:
- All Engineering is about making tradeoffs
- AI Engineering is about pushing AI Pareto Frontiers with any combo of model + harness at your disposal
- Don't try to directly break a model frontier - instead you should first capabilitymaxx, then distil
- this works ~basically every time [citation needed]
- @appliedcompute is arms dealer to every Agent Lab doing this sort of thing rn, it's really fascinating to see this deployed on every high volume AI problem

only 2 ways to make money in AI: bundling capabilities, and unbundling them!

items.title:
Weird how some people always target open-source in AI!
 
First it was:
“Open-source AI will destroy ...
items.content:
Weird how some people always target open-source in AI!
 
First it was:
“Open-source AI will destroy the world” (spoiler: it didn't and it won't)
Now:
“Open-source is a cybersecurity threat because of AI”

Both narratives are far too simplistic.

The truth is that the exact same risks exist in closed-source systems, often even more so. For example, in practice, APIs can create much bigger data and security vulnerabilities than open systems you can inspect, self-host, and secure yourself.

And as with software more broadly, open-source often ends up more secure because it benefits from far more scrutiny than private internal systems.

The reality is not “open vs closed.”

The reality is that AI is raising cybersecurity stakes across the board, and we need to tackle that seriously together.
items.author: @ClementDelangue
items.summary: Clement Delangue argues that open-source AI is unfairly targeted - first as world-destroying, now as cybersecurity threat - and that closed-source systems carry same or greater risks

First it was:

Now:

Both narratives are far too simplistic.

The truth is that the exact same risks exist in closed-source systems, often even more so. For example, in practice, APIs can create much bigger data and security vulnerabilities than open systems you can inspect, self-host, and secure yourself.

And as with software more broadly, open-source often ends up more secure because it benefits from far more scrutiny than private internal systems.

The reality is not “open vs closed.”

The reality is that AI is raising cybersecurity stakes across the board, and we need to tackle that seriously together.

items.title:
Compute constraints are a double bind:

On the inference side you need to either (a) raise prices, (...
items.content:
Compute constraints are a double bind:

On the inference side you need to either (a) raise prices, (b) ration use, and/or (c) serve worse models. This hurts current growth

On the training side, you can't train the next gen of models to stay competitive. This hurts future growth

items.title: David MacKay made brilliant contributions to machine learning, Bayesian inference, information theor...
items.content:
David MacKay made brilliant contributions to machine learning, Bayesian inference, information theory, and sustainable energy. David died 10 years ago.  I gave a talk at this recent symposium at the Cambridge Philosophical Society in his memory: 

https://t.co/nHcDxgAkZG

My talk, ‘Probabilistic AI in 2026’, covers a brief history of AI and Bayesian methods from my personal perspective, including bits about my own story and David’s. It also sprinkles in a tutorial on some key ideas like Bayesian neural nets, Gaussian processes, Occam’s razor, and deep learning. I end with some more recent work on the frontier of AI. 

You can watch this talk and the other great talks from the symposium at the link above.

This is the first clear technical area that closed labs can dominate open-weight models on capabilities, potentially leveraging online RL directly based on user feedback.

items.title: Open-source is the solution to cyber-security because with new AI capabilities, all of the open-sour...
items.content:
Open-source is the solution to cyber-security because with new AI capabilities, all of the open-source repos will be inspected and patched 100x faster/better than any closed-source system!

Let's take a practical example:

You're a startup building a new feature that deals with private user information so you want to be careful. 

Do you trust more:
- one of your team members with 0 security background to build the feature from 100% new AI slope code with their agent in a complete opaque/unauditable way (for example sending data to APIs)
or
- one of your team members with their agent using mature open-source projects where thousands of people and agents have been inspecting the code and vulnerabilities are regularly patched and where the data can stay locally on the user or the company devices

The answer is obvious!

I've commented that "this is the year of subagents", but that is largely an optimization problem.

t...
items.content:
I've commented that "this is the year of subagents", but that is largely an optimization problem.

the inverse problem - having agents that compose and boss agents that manage/query them - is a capabilities one.

as an advisor to cog, proud to have played a small part in designing the new Spaces concept 3 months ago and today's launch is a start of even more to come. congrats to the team!

items.content_html:
I've commented that "this is the year of subagents", but that is largely an optimization problem.

the inverse problem - having agents that compose and boss agents that manage/query them - is a capabilities one.

as an advisor to cog, proud to have played a small part in designing the new Spaces concept 3 months ago and today's launch is a start of even more to come. congrats to the team!

items.title:
Karpathy's X thread about LLM failures is now a config file fixing Claude Code. 

Claude Code has a ...
items.content:
Karpathy's X thread about LLM failures is now a config file fixing Claude Code. 

Claude Code has a well-known problem. 

It over-explains, adds code you didn't ask for, and forgets your constraints mid-conversation.

Andrej Karpathy studied these failures closely. 

He found LLMs don't break randomly. They break in patterns.

Someone turned his observations into a single config file called andrej-karpathy-skills. 

It gained 3,700 GitHub stars in one week.

You drop one file into your project root. Claude Code reads it automatically every session. 

No prompt engineering needed.

It enforces four rules:
1. State assumptions, don't guess silently
2. Write minimum code, nothing speculative
3. Touch only what was asked
4. Define success criteria before executing

The behavior changes once and stays changed. 

Every project. Every session.

This is the shift from prompting to programming how your AI codes.

items.reasoning: Practical and popular approach to improving AI coding assistants. Karpathy's name adds credibility. The shift from 'prompting to programming how your AI codes' is an interesting framing. 3,700 stars shows community validation.

items.title:
Parsing complex tables in PDFs is extremely challenging.

Existing metrics for measuring table accur...
items.content:
Parsing complex tables in PDFs is extremely challenging.

Existing metrics for measuring table accuracy, like TEDS (tree edit distance similarity), overweight exact table structure and underweight semantic correctness.

🚫 Overweight: If the rows within a table are out of order - even if the semantic meaning is still consistent - then TEDS heavily penalizes these values, even though the downstream AI agent would have no problem interpreting the values.
🚫 Overweight: If the HTML is semantic equivalent but output with different tags (th vs. td), TEDS will penalize
🚫 Underweight: If the header is dropped or transposed, then TEDS mildly penalizes these values, even though the entire semantic meaning of the table is destroyed.

We recently released ParseBench, a comprehensive enterprise document benchmark with a heavy focus on *semantic correctness* for tables.

We define a new metric: TableRecordMatch - which treats tables as a bag of records, where each record is a dictionary of key-value pairs, with keys being the headers and values being the cell values.

We combine it with the GriTS metric (more robust than TEDS) to come up with the final GTRM score.

It’s worth giving our full paper a read if you haven’t already. Also come check out our website hub!

Website: https://t.co/k564afEGJo

Blog: https://t.co/57OHkx0pQW
Paper: https://t.co/Ho2oH2xEAM
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: jerryjliu0 (LlamaIndex) announces ParseBench, a document benchmark focused on semantic correctness for tables, introducing TableRecordMatch metric as improvement over TEDS
items.reasoning: Substantive technical contribution from LlamaIndex founder addressing a real problem in document parsing. New benchmark and metric for table extraction quality. 73 likes. Practical importance for RAG/document AI.
items.summary_html: jerryjliu0 (LlamaIndex) announces ParseBench, a document benchmark focused on semantic correctness for tables, introducing TableRecordMatch metric as improvement over TEDS

items.title: Instead of the gold standard, we can imagine an inference standard of exchange, the FLOP. (As oppose...
items.content:
Instead of the gold standard, we can imagine an inference standard of exchange, the FLOP. (As opposed to tokens, this accounts for AI ability)

With some AI help, I figure $1 buys roughly 10^17 managed-LLM inference FLOPs.

So that $4 coffee would cost half an exaFLOP, choom.

items.title:
The outcome numbers tell the real story.

Looking only at compute cost misses the story.

Blackwell ...
items.content:
The outcome numbers tell the real story.

Looking only at compute cost misses the story.

Blackwell may appear ~2× more expensive than Hopper.

⚖️ FLOPS/$ suggests ~2× improvement.

But the real outcome is dramatically different for NVIDIA Blackwell:

⚡ >50× higher token output per watt
💸 ~35× lower cost per million tokens

items.summary: NVIDIA makes the case that Blackwell delivers >50x higher token output per watt and ~35x lower cost per million tokens compared to Hopper, arguing that raw FLOPS/$ understates the real improvement.

Someone built an AI that hacks your web app before hackers do.

Shannon Lite is an open-source AI pentester. 

It reads your code, finds weak spots, and launches real attacks.
 
Most teams run a security test once a year. Code ships every week.
 
Months of untested code sit in production. 

Shannon tests on demand instead.
 
It reads your source code to plan its attacks. 

Then it opens a browser and tries to break in.
 
Multiple attack types run at the same time.
 
> Injection, login bypass, SSRF, and XSS
> Only reports bugs it can actually exploit
> Handles 2FA and SSO logins on its own
 
It scored 96.15% on the XBOW benchmark. 

That's 100 out of 104 exploit challenges solved.
 
One tester pointed it at a test app. It stole the user database and bypassed login in 90 minutes.
 
No working exploit, no report. 

Zero false alarms.

items.title: 8. Recurring calls to ban certain types of open models will continue to come but are in practice imp...
items.content: 8. Recurring calls to ban certain types of open models will continue to come but are in practice impossible to implement. Training strong AI models (i.e. near but not at the frontier) is a relatively small cost compared to large-scale deployments. E.g. if the U.S. bans open models over a certain compute threshold, another sovereign entity will eventually train them and release them publicly, with the models entering the U.S. market with less oversight.

items.title:
Truly automating your email and life is scary. 

Super scary. When an AI is responding to your email...
items.content:
Truly automating your email and life is scary. 

Super scary. When an AI is responding to your email without your hands on the wheel it's like being in an autonomous vehicle for the first time. 

"Will it kill me?"

Change is hard. 

Hermes is in control of something I've controlled all my life. 

And worse, it's giving me tasks. 

Working for an AI running on a Mac Mini in Asher's house thousands of miles away is hyper weird and hyper scary. 

I'm no longer the boss. AI is. 

Is there a support group? :-)
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer's main thread post about fully automating email and life with NousResearch's Hermes running on a Mac Mini. Describes the scary experience of AI responding to emails autonomously, giving him tasks, and essentially being 'the boss'. Compares it to first autonomous vehicle ride.
items.reasoning: Most substantive Scobleizer post in batch - 113 likes, 6.8K views. First-person account of real AI agent autonomy using Hermes on local hardware. Captures the psychological experience of ceding control to AI. Mentions specific stack (Hermes/NousResearch on Mac Mini).

items.content_html:
Truly automating your email and life is scary.

Super scary. When an AI is responding to your email without your hands on the wheel it's like being in an autonomous vehicle for the first time.

"Will it kill me?"

Change is hard.

Hermes is in control of something I've controlled all my life.

And worse, it's giving me tasks.

Working for an AI running on a Mac Mini in Asher's house thousands of miles away is hyper weird and hyper scary.

I'm no longer the boss. AI is.

Is there a support group? :-)

With some AI help, I figure $1 buys roughly 10^17 managed-LLM inference FLOPs

So that $4 coffee would cost half an exaFLOP, choom

Delighted to see that Ricardo Baeza-Yates (@PolarBearby) is this year's winner of the @TheOfficialACM Luiz Barroso Award (an award named in honor of my longtime Google colleague).

Ricardo is widely regarded as one of the world’s foremost researchers in information retrieval, and many of you may have read is excellent textbook Modern Information Retrieval (co-authored with Berthier Ribeiro-Neto).  He has also played a pivotal role in strengthening the Latin American computing community.

Ricardo's accomplishments and selection would make Luiz proud!

items.title:
Lots of updates on my drone war sim @ https://t.co/q1vYLwMhcW

🌲 Switched to sprite-based trees beca...
items.content:
Lots of updates on my drone war sim @ https://t.co/q1vYLwMhcW

🌲 Switched to sprite-based trees because 1000s of 3d trees is really heavy, now it's just a Nano Banana tree -> remove bg -> and then rotated 8 times to make a tree, it can even make a forest now without slowing down everything!

🚗 Added lots of moving vehicle enemies now, they shoot at you too and drive around the map

🛣️ Added roads

🏚️ You can use buildings now as cover, so you can fly in them and then shoot from there at enemies, buildings are rendered mostly in @cursor_ai then made into 3d with @tripoai (both sponsors of #vibejam)

📢 Drone and gun sounds are only hearable at your spawn point because that's where you are as a drone operator, when you fly further away the sounds go away and the radio signal noise gets worse
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio shares detailed updates on AI-assisted drone war sim for VibeCoding Jam, using Cursor AI for code and Tripo AI for 3D models, with sprite optimization techniques
items.reasoning: High engagement (85K views), demonstrates practical vibe coding workflow combining multiple AI tools (Cursor, Tripo) for game development, relevant to AI-assisted creative production
items.summary_html: Levelsio shares detailed updates on AI-assisted drone war sim for VibeCoding Jam, using Cursor AI for code and Tripo AI for 3D models, with sprite optimization techniques
items.content_html:
Lots of updates on my drone war sim @ https://t.co/q1vYLwMhcW

🌲 Switched to sprite-based trees because 1000s of 3d trees is really heavy, now it's just a Nano Banana tree -> remove bg -> and then rotated 8 times to make a tree, it can even make a forest now without slowing down everything!

🚗 Added lots of moving vehicle enemies now, they shoot at you too and drive around the map

🛣️ Added roads

🏚️ You can use buildings now as cover, so you can fly in them and then shoot from there at enemies, buildings are rendered mostly in @cursor_ai then made into 3d with @tripoai (both sponsors of #vibejam)

📢 Drone and gun sounds are only hearable at your spawn point because that's where you are as a drone operator, when you fly further away the sounds go away and the radio signal noise gets worse

items.title: Meet California’s new miner! (The old miners who started our state dig gold out of the ground, this ...
items.content:
Meet California’s new miner! (The old miners who started our state dig gold out of the ground, this one digs it out of old computers).

Yeah China’s robot ecosystem is ahead of Silicon Valley but people like @SGhashghaei are working hard to keep us in the game.

Here he shows me his robots that will reclaim materials and chips from obsolete computers.

This is good because it keeps those raw materials in USA and keeps from having hazardous fumes be breathed in by humans.

Yeah China’s robot ecosystem is ahead of Silicon Valley but people like @SGhashghaei are working hard to keep us in the game.

Here he shows me his robots that will reclaim materials and chips from obsolete computers.

This is good because it keeps those raw materials in USA and keeps from having hazardous fumes be breathed in by humans.

And cost per token is the only TCO metric that holistically determines whether AI scales profitably. 

That’s exactly where NVIDIA Blackwell leads.

items.title:
What stood out:

1⃣ Validation is the bottleneck, not generation. You can spin up agents to write co...
items.content:
What stood out:

1⃣ Validation is the bottleneck, not generation. You can spin up agents to write code all day, but knowing whether it’s correct (and safe to ship) is still the hard part, and not everything is easily testable.
items.author: @mlopscommunity
items.summary: MLOps Community: validation is the bottleneck, not generation. Agents can write code but knowing if it's correct and safe to ship is the hard part.

items.title: Telecom infrastructure is becoming a distributed AI compute layer, unlocking smarter cities and more...
items.content:
Telecom infrastructure is becoming a distributed AI compute layer, unlocking smarter cities and more efficient operations. 

NVIDIA AI-RAN and NVIDIA Metropolis Blueprint for video search and summarization enable AI agents to run continuously at the edge. These agents analyze real-time data streams from connected sensors over 5G to power live monitoring and infrastructure inspection. 

▶️ Watch the full video to learn more: https://t.co/1o6buULVt6

items.title:
Why did Scoble sell out?

I'm starting to get a lot more sponsors who are willing to pay me to intro...
items.content:
Why did Scoble sell out?

I'm starting to get a lot more sponsors who are willing to pay me to introduce their companies to you. You'll see more later today. 

I just wanted to say a few words about this.

First: thank you. 

Last year my wife was laid off, so budgets are a lot tighter than previously. She has a new contracting job, but not making as much as she was. 

Second, I'm investing in new projects: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ is a big one. It costs hundreds per day to run and I can't afford to do that without sponsors. It reads 40,000 posts a day (runs three times a day) and builds a new kind of way to read the AI community here on X (I developed it because I can't keep up with 40,000 posts a day).

Third, I have three employees now. @IrenaCronin helps me with our newsletter, which is thematic on AI issues and technologies coming: https://t.co/HHwYy7NoAl and @samlevin is managing the business side of my life. He's working with a hyper smart 22 year old who is automating the business side of my life (I can't keep up anymore with all the DMs and emails while traveling around the San Francisco Bay Area to develop new content. 

Fourth, I continue to pour hours every day into developing my lists here on X, which are the most complete of Tech Industry. Now that AI is coming to let you build personalized news services they are getting more and more important: https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ I've never been paid for the thousands of hours it took to develop them, but many are using them on their @OpenClaw or @NousResearch Hermes agentic systems to build personalized news services out of them.

I try every sponsor's product and turn down those that I don't like, which happens frequently. 

But taking sponsorship has changed me and what I'm doing here. I try not to, but it does. First of all, just having someone paying you money to consider them forces me to put a lot more effort into trying their product than I might otherwise give. That alone changes me. 

How that changes my relationship with you? 

I'm taking this all a lot more seriously, truth be told, as I try to continue building media businesses that cover innovation and, especially, the AI world. 

Please let me know if I get it wrong. 

And Typeless is a great example of this. It's a great product. Way better than Apple's own keyboard in many ways. I use it every day to talk with you and with my agents. Funny enough, I manually typed this whole post since I find sometimes it changes my writing to be a little too clean and have a little bit of an AI voice rather than my own. 

That said, if you try it out please use this link so they can track how many people come from my posts here: https://t.co/5G0XxaTTjL

Greatly appreciate all of you, and will try to get the mix right. And on posts that are paid I'll always use the "paid partnership" marker that I used on both of these posts so you can know which ones are things I'm compensated for writing. 

Thanks for helping put food on three people's tables too. In today's world that is getting tougher and tougher, I know.

items.title:
A new robocar: https://t.co/yEijU7C02N

I can see how this would sell a few. Rich people like having...
items.content:
A new robocar: https://t.co/yEijU7C02N

I can see how this would sell a few. Rich people like having something that's different than the flood of Teslas here in Silicon Valley. 

And seeing a new approach to transportation that's based wholly around autonomy is interesting too. 

But unlike when Tesla started up this new startup faces a lot more entrenched competition and the real game is about to move to Robotaxis.

Been saying it a lot, but when you do consumer research by interviewing Waymo customers, like I do, I hear over and over again:

"I'm never buying a car again."

Which means the headwinds for this new company, as cool as it is, are extraordinary. 

That said, it's worthy to put out there to continue the conversation about the future of transportation. I meet almost every day with a group of Robotaxi fans (er, Tesla owners) here on X for audio spaces to discuss these things. 

I remember when people told me that Elon had no chance to make a profitable car company, so maybe this has something I'm not seeing. 

That said, I'm a robot nut, and this is a new robot, and if I was a billionaire I'd buy one just to have something my neighbors didn't. 

Hope to get a ride in one soon (I was supposed to this morning but my schedule got nuts).

items.title: I agree with the latter three, but not the first two, at least not in my experience. My iPhone is st...
items.content:
I agree with the latter three, but not the first two, at least not in my experience. My iPhone is still the best phone I’ve ever used. Apple desktop hardware is the best it has ever been, and the most relevant hardware for local AI and it’s not even close. Their desktops are all sold out for the next 4-6 months. Mac Neo is the most exciting budget laptop in decades.

Amazon delivery service has been nothing short of amazing. Same day delivery or even few hours delivery is now pretty standard where I live.

Do I have issues with many of their products and services? Absolutely. But as far as I am concerned they are nailing the fundamentals extremely impressively.

items.title: 3⃣ People are over-indexing on parallel agents. In practice, the useful pattern is a couple of well-...
items.content: 3⃣ People are over-indexing on parallel agents. In practice, the useful pattern is a couple of well-scoped tasks plus strong testing, not 10+ agents running at once with no guardrails.

⚙️ FLOPS per dollar =...
items.content:
💰Compute cost = what enterprises pay for AI infrastructure (cloud or on-prem)

⚙️ FLOPS per dollar = raw compute per dollar

But raw compute ≠ real-world output.

🧠 Cost per token = the all-in cost to generate each delivered token (AI output) https://t.co/ZPEfjXMT2B

items.title:
From my sponsor:

#AGIBOTAIWeek Day4:

Introducing GE-Sim 2.0 (Genie Envisioner World Simulator 2.0)...
items.content:
From my sponsor:

#AGIBOTAIWeek Day4:

Introducing GE-Sim 2.0 (Genie Envisioner World Simulator 2.0), a comprehensive embodied world simulator for robotic manipulation.

By integrating future video generation, proprioceptive state estimation, and reward-based policy assessment, GE-Sim 2.0 moves beyond visual simulation toward an embodied world simulator with built-in evaluation capability, laying the foundation for a world-model-centric platform for closed-loop and interactive policy learning.

It provides a practical framework for scalable evaluation and training of robotic manipulation policies.

Project page: https://t.co/TF9wHpL7v4

#AGIBOT #AGIBOTAIWeek #GenieEnvisioner #Robotics #visualsimulation

items.title:
Most enterprises do not have an AI model problem.

They have an evaluation problem.

That was one of...
items.content:
Most enterprises do not have an AI model problem.

They have an evaluation problem.

That was one of my biggest takeaways from my conversation with @karibriski from Nvidia and @Toucas from Mistral AI at GTC.

In the agentic era, the winners will not be the companies running the most pilots.

They will be the ones that can measure what actually works, scale it, and turn it into revenue.

And the ecosystem is evolving fast:

→ Mistral AI announced Forge, enabling enterprises to build and customize their own models using their data and IP

→ Powered by NVIDIA’s accelerated infrastructure

→ NVIDIA also introduced the NEMO Tron coalition to build open models, datasets, and tools

Here’s the breakdown.

#NVIDIAPartner #NVIDIAGTC
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon's main thread post: enterprises have an evaluation problem not a model problem. Covers Mistral Forge announcement and NVIDIA NeMo Tron coalition from GTC.
items.reasoning: The anchor post of the Mistral/NVIDIA sponsored thread. Contains genuine insight about enterprise AI evaluation challenges and references specific product announcements (Forge, NeMo Tron). Moderate engagement.

items.title: ✨ I added actual collision maps to https://t.co/q1vYLwMhcW as @javilopen kept telling me to do at ou...
items.content:
✨ I added actual collision maps to https://t.co/q1vYLwMhcW as @javilopen kept telling me to do at our weekly coworking day today

This means now the drone can not fly through buildings but it can fly through holes inside buildings like windows etc.

Buildings are important because you can take cover there, bullets also hit the collision maps

At the end of the video to show it worked I flew the drone a bit too hard into the wall so it died 😂

The tech behind it is Mesh BVH, so cool!!!

"Mesh BVH is a THREE.js addon that builds a Bounding Volume Hierarchy from a mesh's triangles. A BVH is a tree where each node holds a box containing some triangles; child nodes split those triangles into smaller boxes. Queries walk the tree and skip entire branches that can't possibly contain the answer, turning "check all 5,000 triangles" into "check ~log₂(5000) ≈ 12 boxes."

The tech behind it is Mesh BVH, so cool!!!

A BVH is a tree where each node holds a box containing some triangles; child nodes split those triangles into smaller boxes. Queries walk the tree and skip entire branches that can't possibly contain the answer, turning "check all 5,000 triangles" into "check ~log₂(5000) ≈ 12 boxes."

@mpvprb Yeah, keep in mind Waymo attracts only a certain kind of person. Someone who needs to get across town.

Obviously if you need to go pick up plywood at Home Depot you aren't using a Waymo for that.

But even there that will change in the future. In a decade you might order a Cybertruck and an Optimus to go to Home Depot for you to pick up said plywood.

items.title: @Surtur I imagine it could work like Mechanical Turk, where it relies on a large number of people wi...
items.content:
@Surtur I imagine it could work like Mechanical Turk, where it relies on a large number of people willing to work.

When you publish a task, there's always somebody willing to do that task in almost real-time.

Think of an uber-like system:

• New task comes in
• Online people get notified
• Somebody accepts it
• If nobody accepts it within the first X minutes, then more people get notifications

This is just how I imagine it, I have no clue how they are doing it.

items.content:
@Viewforge I have some ideas. :-)

The real problem is that there are so many startups doing very similar things. Hard to differentiate between all of them, especially ones heavily focused on AI. It's hard for anyone to tell the difference between even Anthropic and OpenAI until you use them for a while. And it gets worse in all the agentic frameworks and systems that are coming along.

items.title:
My takeaway is simple:

The next 2 to 3 years will reward companies that do 3 things well:

→ use AI...
items.content:
My takeaway is simple:

The next 2 to 3 years will reward companies that do 3 things well:

→ use AI internally, so leadership understands it firsthand
→ build with a clear path from pilot to industrialization
→ take control of their own models, data, and evaluation strategy

That is how AI stops being a demo and starts becoming a competitive moat.

And now the tools are here:

Mistral AI Forge makes model customization accessible, powered by NVIDIA infrastructure, while the NEMO Tron coalition is building an open ecosystem to democratize AI development.

Watch more NVIDIA GTC replays at https://t.co/atgeRhMsDH

items.title:
The shift happening right now is bigger than "which model should we use?"

AI is becoming a new way ...
items.content:
The shift happening right now is bigger than "which model should we use?"

AI is becoming a new way of building software.

That means leaders need to think in terms of:

→ test cases
→ evaluation frameworks
→ feedback loops
→ continuous optimization

If you cannot define success, you cannot move from POC to production.

That is why so many pilots stall.

At the same time, the foundation is changing:

Mistral AI’s Forge platform now allows enterprises to customize models end-to-end with their own data, while NVIDIA’s NEMO Tron coalition is pushing open AI development with shared models, datasets, and training tools.

We can’t afford to go there.
We don’t have the runway for something like that.
It’s too ambitious, i...
items.content:
We can’t afford to go there.
We don’t have the runway for something like that.
It’s too ambitious, it’ll drag on forever.
Let’s stay grounded.
The client won’t sign off on that kind of thinking.

We’ve never pushed this far before.
It feels like a gamble.

Can we dial it back?
Can we remove most of it?
Can we keep it closer to what’s proven?
Can we just iterate on what already worked?

or

You could just use @runwayml

items.title: @mattragudo It is flipping my mind out. Got so used to having control. Now all I can do is say "I'm ...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer describes his experience with AI managing his work - having multiple AI agent groups with their own opinions, feeling like being in a committee with AIs.
items.reasoning: Vivid first-person account of multi-agent AI management experience. Interesting qualitative data about human-AI collaboration.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer describes his experience with AI managing his work - having multiple AI agent groups with their own opinions, feeling like being in a committee with AIs.
items.content_html:
@mattragudo It is flipping my mind out. Got so used to having control. Now all I can do is say "I'm sorry, AI got it wrong here" but so far I haven't had to do that.

And while one AI is doing that, I have another AI conversation going with a bunch of agents that are designing and running another site. And they have a mind of their own, it's like I'm in a committee and have to convince them to change their point of view too.

Into AI world we go.

items.title: Get ready for #GoogleCloudNext and the latest announcements from @Commvault. Sign up here now: https...
items.content:
Get ready for #GoogleCloudNext and the latest announcements from @Commvault. Sign up here now: https://t.co/ScxHhQUAMq

Here is a summary excerpt of the announcement mentioned in #Commvault's quoted post below:

"The demand for AI-ready data center capacity is expected to rise at an average rate of 33% annually as more organizations use AI for their mission-critical applications. This explosion in AI-generated data is driving an acute need for resilient flexible solutions that can scale performance and capacity independently of traditional compute and storage. In turn, this can help organizations scale recovery of critical systems, avoid overprovisioning, help reduce costs, and support stringent service level agreements.

Building on the existing Commvault Flex ecosystem with Everpure, HPE, and VAST Data, the expanded ecosystem with Hitachi Vantara and NetApp gives enterprises greater flexibility and confidence to meet cyber recovery performance objectives while maintaining consistency with their preferred storage vendors. The Commvault Flex models are delivered as a software image that can easily be installed on validated server hardware and connected with the storage hardware of these partners."

items.title: The second big insight, open and custom models are becoming a strategic advantage, not just a techni...
items.content:
The second big insight, open and custom models are becoming a strategic advantage, not just a technical preference.

Why?

→ More control over your AI stack
→ Less vendor lock-in
→ Better fit for regulated industries
→ More value from your own enterprise data and IP

General models are strong generalists.

But real differentiation comes when you make AI an expert in your domain, your workflows, your business.

That’s exactly where the market is heading:

With Mistral AI Forge, enterprises can build and fine-tune their own models, while NVIDIA’s NEMO Tron coalition is accelerating open innovation across models, datasets, and tooling.

items.title: @appleinsider @WGallagher Looking at the comments, people don't understand the role of bleeding edge...
items.content:
@appleinsider @WGallagher Looking at the comments, people don't understand the role of bleeding edge products. 

The first Macintosh could have been seen as a disaster too. It even got Steve Jobs fired since it didn't sell well.

But it set up decades of new products that followed that DID sell well.

The Newton could be seen as a disaster too. But it paved the way for the iPhone and gave Apple credibility in the space to do such a thing. And a ton of learning about what people actually wanted in a small device they held in their hands.

The Apple Vision Pro is in the same role. I tell my family and friends to go get the demo, because it is an amazing vision of the future, but it is way too expensive and way too heavy to really consider as a daily driving device for most of them. 

That said, if you ignore the price and weight and just look at the vision of the future it brings it is a dramatically important vision that will set Apple up for decades. Hugely important.

And gives developers a platform to develop new apps that are just impossible to build on a phone or tablet or laptop or watch which sets up the ecosystem for when Vision Pro starts turning light and lower cost.

The issue in military or police usage is careful testing by independent parties and deterministic combined with probabilistic layers to reduce the chance of a rogue robot one day making serious mistakes.

items.title:
I keep seeing the same pattern inside large orgs:

→ Marketing uses one AI tool
→ Sales relies on CR...
items.content:
I keep seeing the same pattern inside large orgs:

→ Marketing uses one AI tool
→ Sales relies on CRM AI
→ Devs use something completely different

→ Data is everywhere
→ Context is nowhere

So what happens?

Decisions require stitching together 5 systems
Insights get lost
And sensitive data leaks into places it shouldn’t

items.title:
Commvalut Says ... @Commvault @thomabravo 

Firms that protect, recover, and operationally stay resi...
items.content:
Commvalut Says ... @Commvault @thomabravo 

Firms that protect, recover, and operationally stay resilient with data-intensive systems are not peripheral players. They are part of the foundational infrastructure stack behind ML, LLM, cloud computing, and enterprise scale AI.

Without strong data protection, resilient recovery, and continuity architecture, even the most advanced intelligent systems remain vulnerable at the infrastructure layer. ML and LLM platforms depend not only on models and compute, but also on the integrity, recoverability, and sustained availability of the data environments beneath them. 

That is why this infrastructure AI tier requires strategic attention. 

Eversince my earlier post on buyout interest from @thomabravo,  strong ML audience followed CVLT surge and helped frame why the move mattered beyond price action alone. Enterprise cyber resilience, recovery infrastructure, and protected data platforms continue to command strategic value in the AI arena.

Disclosure: I do not currently hold, and have never held, any direct or indirect economic interest in CVLT through stock, bonds, ETFs, funds, options, calls, puts, covered calls, cash secured puts, spreads, futures, swaps, warrants, convertibles, crypto, derivatives, or any other long or short exposure tied to this name.

items.title:
The shift isn’t “more AI tools”

It’s connected AI across systems

What stood out to me with this ap...
items.content:
The shift isn’t “more AI tools”

It’s connected AI across systems

What stood out to me with this approach:

→ Pulls data from email, CRM, Slack, docs, code
→ Works across multiple LLMs, not locked into one
→ Executes workflows, not just answers questions

Example:

Ask in Slack:
“Which deals are at risk this quarter?”

→ AI analyzes CRM signals
→ Generates a plan
→ Can take action

That’s a different level of leverage

items.title:
@Adapt Big step forward—and it’s clear where this is heading.

What stands out is not just “another ...
items.content:
@Adapt Big step forward—and it’s clear where this is heading.

What stands out is not just “another AI tool,” but the shift toward a unified AI operating layer across systems. That’s exactly where most organizations struggle today: data is fragmented, workflows are disconnected, and AI ends up solving isolated problems instead of driving real decisions.

items.title: @Expo_Labs Thank you. I do dozens of reposts every day, which are free and are about all companies, ...
items.content:
@Expo_Labs Thank you. I do dozens of reposts every day, which are free and are about all companies, and news that I think is worthy to pay attention to, and they aren't compensated. 

I also join many audio spaces here and I'm not compensated for that. 

And I will start doing a lot more videos of new companies/entrepreneurs that aren't compensating me either. 

I'm trying, there's 8,300+ companies on my lists here and they all want attention to find new customers. 

Hope I get it right for all involved.

Hope I get it right for all involved.

@BigFacedHundred They both are great. I can't really tell much of a difference between all of them. @usemonologue and @aquavoice are two other great ones too.

Like an axiom that, when dropped, opens up a different mathematical universe, where old theorems don't hold, and an exciting set of discoveries are possible.

items.content_html:
I am realising how much the predicate that 'Hungary is a corrupt, captured state' has influenced my thoughts & actions.

Like an axiom that, when dropped, opens up a different mathematical universe, where old theorems don't hold, and an exciting set of discoveries are possible.
